stories.title: OpenAI raises $110B at $840B valuation
stories.why_it_matters: The largest private funding round in history signals that the AI arms race has entered a new phase where only companies with access to tens of billions in capital can compete at the frontier.

sections.title: OpenAI raises $110B at $840B valuation
sections.summary: OpenAI is raising $110 billion from Amazon, SoftBank, and Nvidia, reaching an $840 billion valuation before an expected IPO.
sections.condensed: OpenAI is raising $110 billion from Amazon, SoftBank, and Nvidia, valuing the company at $840 billion ahead of an expected IPO later this year. This massive capital injection will secure the advanced AI chips and computing capacity needed to maintain OpenAI's industry lead against competitors like Google and Anthropic.
sections.standard:
OpenAI is raising $110 billion from Amazon, SoftBank, and Nvidia, valuing the company at $840 billion ahead of an expected IPO later this year. This massive capital injection will secure the advanced AI chips and computing capacity needed to maintain OpenAI's industry lead against competitors like Google and Anthropic.

The deal includes Amazon investing $50 billion, SoftBank contributing $30 billion, and Nvidia adding $30 billion. As part of the arrangement, OpenAI struck a deal to utilize two gigawatts of computing capacity powered by Amazon's Trainium AI chips, while expanding their cloud partnership to spend an additional $100 billion on Amazon Web Services over eight years.

This is the largest private funding round in history, signaling that the AI arms race has entered a new phase where only companies with access to tens of billions in capital can compete at the frontier. The sheer scale of investment reflects the enormous computational demands of training and running next-generation AI models.
sections.expanded:
OpenAI is raising $110 billion in a funding round that values the ChatGPT maker at $840 billion, making it the largest private funding round in history. The investment comes from three major technology players: Amazon ($50 billion), SoftBank ($30 billion), and Nvidia ($30 billion). This massive capital injection is designed to secure the advanced AI chips and computing capacity OpenAI needs to maintain its industry lead against competitors like Google and Anthropic.

As part of the deal, OpenAI struck a landmark agreement to utilize two gigawatts of computing capacity powered by Amazon's Trainium AI chips, while expanding their cloud partnership to spend an additional $100 billion on Amazon Web Services (AWS) over eight years. AWS will serve as the exclusive third-party cloud provider for OpenAI Frontier, a new enterprise platform for building and running AI agents. However, Microsoft Azure remains the exclusive cloud provider for OpenAI's API access, and OpenAI's first-party products will continue to be hosted on Azure.

The timing of this raise is strategically critical. OpenAI recently launched GPT-5.4, its most advanced model to date, featuring a one-million token context window and native agentic capabilities. ChatGPT currently boasts over 900 million weekly active users, while the AI-assisted coding product Codex has grown to 1.6 million weekly users. The company is expected to pursue an IPO later this year.

The competitive landscape is intensifying rapidly. Google's Gemini 2.5 Pro offers a two-million token context window, while Chinese AI lab DeepSeek's V3.2 model delivers comparable quality at a fraction of the cost ($0.28 per million input tokens versus OpenAI's $2.50). Meanwhile, Anthropic faces federal pushback after being designated a 'supply chain risk' by the Pentagon over AI safety guardrail disputes.

This funding round fundamentally reshapes the AI industry's economics. The sheer scale of investment — more than many countries' GDP — signals that the AI arms race has entered a new phase where only companies with access to tens of billions in capital can compete at the frontier. It also deepens the entanglement between Big Tech and AI labs, with Amazon, Microsoft, and Nvidia all holding significant stakes in OpenAI's future.

stories.title: Pentagon blacklists Anthropic over AI safety guardrail disputes
stories.why_it_matters: This unprecedented clash between a leading AI company and the US government sets a dangerous precedent for whether private companies or the military will control the ethical boundaries of AI.

sections.title: Pentagon blacklists Anthropic over AI safety guardrail disputes
sections.summary: The Pentagon designated Anthropic a "supply chain risk" after the company restricted its AI from autonomous weapons.
sections.condensed: The Pentagon designated Anthropic a supply chain risk and blocked federal agencies from using its tools after CEO Dario Amodei refused to allow Claude's use in autonomous weapons or domestic surveillance. In response, Anthropic filed federal lawsuits alleging retaliation and First Amendment violations, arguing that the government is attempting to punish the company for its AI safety guardrails. This clash highlights a critical industry battle over whether private tech companies or the government will dictate the ethical boundaries of powerful AI infrastructure.
sections.standard:
The Pentagon designated Anthropic a supply chain risk and blocked federal agencies from using its tools after CEO Dario Amodei refused to allow Claude's use in autonomous weapons or domestic surveillance. In response, Anthropic filed federal lawsuits alleging retaliation and First Amendment violations, arguing that the government is attempting to punish the company for its AI safety guardrails.

This highly unusual designation — a legal mechanism typically reserved for foreign adversaries like Huawei or Kaspersky — was deployed against an American AI company for the first time. Following the designation, President Trump announced that all federal agencies would cease using Anthropic's tools. The Pentagon quickly cleared rival models, including Elon Musk's xAI and OpenAI's ChatGPT, for use in classified systems.

The dispute represents a fundamental struggle over governance and who will control the most powerful general-purpose technology of the decade. If the government enforces rules demanding unrestricted military access, AI companies fear their infrastructure will become an extension of the national security state. Conversely, if companies maintain strict control, private corporations will dictate the operational boundaries of national defense.
sections.expanded:
The confrontation between Anthropic and the U.S. government erupted after the Pentagon designated the American AI company a "supply chain risk," effectively blacklisting it from federal contracts. This highly unusual designation — a legal mechanism typically reserved for foreign adversaries like Huawei or Kaspersky — was deployed after Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei refused to loosen the company's safety guardrails to allow its Claude AI model to be used for lethal autonomous weapons or mass domestic surveillance. The Pentagon countered that private companies should not dictate how the military utilizes technology, insisting that models must be available for "all lawful uses" in warfare and tactical operations.

In response to the blacklist, Anthropic filed two federal lawsuits against the Trump administration, alleging that the government illegally retaliated against the company and violated its First Amendment rights. The lawsuits seek to block the enforcement of the designation, with Anthropic arguing that the government is trying to destroy the economic value of the company to punish it for adhering to a protected viewpoint on AI safety. Following the designation, President Trump announced that all federal agencies would cease using Anthropic's tools.

This dispute has significantly altered the competitive landscape of the AI industry. Before the feud, Anthropic was the first frontier AI lab permitted for use on classified government networks, and it had partnered with defense contractors like Palantir to assist in processing complex data for government officials. With Anthropic sidelined, the Pentagon quickly cleared rival models, including Elon Musk's xAI and OpenAI's ChatGPT, for use in classified systems. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman publicly called the government's actions an "extremely scary precedent," yet his company signed a deal with the Department of War shortly after Anthropic's removal.

Strategically, this clash highlights a critical tension facing the entire artificial intelligence industry: balancing rapid innovation and business incentives with ethical responsibilities. The situation represents a fundamental struggle over governance and who will control the most powerful general-purpose technology of the decade. If the government enforces rules demanding unrestricted military access, AI companies fear their infrastructure will simply become an extension of the national security state. Conversely, if companies maintain strict control, private corporations will dictate the operational boundaries of national defense. Ultimately, the Anthropic blacklist sets a profound precedent regarding the executive branch's power to penalize domestic technology firms over their product safety policies.

stories.title: OpenAI launches GPT-5.4 with native agentic and coding capabilities
stories.why_it_matters: GPT-5.4 marks the transition from AI chatbots to autonomous AI agents, with native computer use and coding capabilities that could reshape enterprise software development.

sections.title: OpenAI launches GPT-5.4 with native agentic and coding capabilities
sections.summary: On March 5, 2026, OpenAI launched GPT-5.4, unifying advanced reasoning, native computer use, and coding capabilities.
sections.condensed: OpenAI launched GPT-5.4, featuring a one-million token context window, native agentic coding capabilities, and reasoning that outperforms competitors. The model costs $2.50 per million input tokens and is supported by a massive $110 billion funding round to expand computing infrastructure. This launch and capital raise cement OpenAI's dominance as the tech industry pivots rapidly toward autonomous enterprise AI agents.
sections.standard:
OpenAI launched GPT-5.4 on March 5, 2026, a model that unifies reasoning, coding, and native agentic capabilities. It doubles its predecessor's context window to one million tokens and introduces native computer use ability, alongside a tool search feature that reduces token usage by 47%. Priced at $2.50 per million input tokens, GPT-5.4 outperformed competitors in key benchmarks, scoring 83% on GDPval compared to Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.6 at 78%.

To secure the computing power required for these advanced AI agents, OpenAI is raising $110 billion at an $840 billion valuation, with major investments from Amazon ($50 billion), SoftBank ($30 billion), and Nvidia ($30 billion). AWS will serve as the exclusive third-party cloud provider for OpenAI Frontier, a new enterprise platform for building AI agents.

The competitive context remains intense, as OpenAI faces high-end reasoning rivals like Anthropic's Claude 4.6 and Google's Gemini 2.5 Pro, which features an industry-leading two-million token context window. Simultaneously, OpenAI faces extreme pricing pressure from Chinese AI labs, notably DeepSeek, whose V3.2 model offers high quality at a breakthrough price of $0.28 per million input tokens.
sections.expanded:
On March 5, 2026, OpenAI released GPT-5.4, a highly anticipated model that unifies reasoning, coding, and native agentic capabilities. This model doubles its predecessor's context window to one million tokens and introduces native computer use ability, alongside a tool search feature that reduces token usage by 47%. Priced at $2.50 per million input tokens and $10.00 per million output tokens, GPT-5.4 outperformed competitors in key benchmarks, scoring 83% on GDPval compared to Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.6 at 78%, and achieving 57.7% on SWE-Bench Pro.

To secure the computing power required for these advanced AI agents, OpenAI is raising a staggering $110 billion in a funding round that values the ChatGPT maker at $840 billion. Major investors in this blockbuster round include Amazon ($50 billion), SoftBank ($30 billion), and Nvidia ($30 billion). Through this round, OpenAI struck a deal to utilize two gigawatts of computing capacity powered by Amazon's Trainium AI chips, while expanding their cloud partnership to spend an additional $100 billion on Amazon Web Services (AWS) over eight years. AWS will serve as the exclusive third-party cloud provider for OpenAI Frontier, a new enterprise platform for building and running AI agents. However, Microsoft Azure remains the exclusive cloud provider for OpenAI's API access, and OpenAI's first-party products will continue to be hosted on Azure.

Strategically, the GPT-5.4 launch and the massive capital influx solidify OpenAI's position in an increasingly fierce and costly global AI race. The industry is seeing a major shift toward long-running, autonomous agents capable of executing complex workflows, which require immense computational infrastructure. OpenAI's tools are already seeing massive adoption; ChatGPT currently boasts over 900 million weekly active users, while the AI-assisted coding product Codex has grown to 1.6 million weekly users.

The competitive context remains intense, as OpenAI is fending off high-end reasoning rivals like Anthropic's Claude 4.6 and Google's Gemini 2.5 Pro, which features an industry-leading two-million token context window. Simultaneously, OpenAI faces extreme pricing pressure from Chinese AI labs, notably DeepSeek, whose V3.2 model offers high quality at a breakthrough price of $0.28 per million input tokens. Furthermore, as competitors like Anthropic face federal pushback and Pentagon blacklisting over AI safety and military use policies, OpenAI has been cleared for use in classified government systems, opening additional strategic pathways for the company's enterprise and defense applications.

stories.title: Nvidia unveils Vera Rubin and Feynman computing platforms
stories.why_it_matters: Nvidia's next-generation architectures cement the company as the indispensable infrastructure provider for the entire AI industry, from cloud hyperscalers to space operations.

sections.title: Nvidia unveils Vera Rubin and Feynman computing platforms
sections.summary: Nvidia unveiled Vera Rubin and Feynman, two next-generation, full-stack computing platforms designed for agentic AI infrastructure.
sections.condensed: At GTC 2026, Nvidia introduced Vera Rubin, a fully integrated platform featuring seven new chips, including the Vera CPU and BlueField-4 STX. Nvidia also previewed its successor, the Feynman architecture, which is powered by the Rosa CPU and LP40 LPU to advance AI factory compute and networking. Major players like AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Thinking Machines Lab are already adopting these architectures to build gigawatt-scale data centers.
sections.standard:
At GTC 2026, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang announced Vera Rubin and Feynman, two next-generation platforms designed specifically for the era of agentic AI. Vera Rubin is a comprehensive architecture comprising seven chips, five rack-scale systems, and a supercomputer, featuring the new Nvidia Vera CPU and BlueField-4 STX storage architecture. The platform also extends beyond Earth with Space-1 Vera Rubin, designed for AI data center compute in orbit for geospatial intelligence and autonomous space operations.

The Feynman architecture follows Rubin and is designed to advance every pillar of the AI factory — compute, memory, storage, networking, and security. Feynman will be driven by the Rosa CPU, engineered to efficiently move data, tools, and tokens across agentic AI infrastructure, paired with the LP40 LPU and BlueField-5 networking.

Major industry players are already heavily investing. AWS plans to deploy the Rubin architecture as part of an expansion that includes over one million Nvidia GPUs. Microsoft Azure was the first hyperscale provider to power up Vera Rubin NVL72 systems. Thinking Machines Lab announced a partnership to deploy at least one gigawatt of Vera Rubin systems for training frontier models.
sections.expanded:
At GTC 2026, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang announced the company's next massive leaps in accelerated computing infrastructure: the Vera Rubin and Feynman platforms. Designed specifically for the era of agentic AI, these architectures represent a strategic push toward extreme hardware-software codesign and vertically integrated full-stack systems.

The Vera Rubin platform is a comprehensive architecture comprising seven chips, five rack-scale systems, and a supercomputer. It features the new Nvidia Vera CPU and the BlueField-4 STX storage architecture. Emphasizing full-stack integration, Huang described Vera Rubin as a giant, vertically integrated system optimized end-to-end. The Rubin architecture is also set to extend beyond Earth; Nvidia introduced Space-1 Vera Rubin, designed to bring AI data center compute into orbit for geospatial intelligence and autonomous space operations.

Following Rubin is the Feynman architecture, designed to advance every pillar of the AI factory, including compute, memory, storage, networking, and security. Feynman will be driven by the new Rosa CPU — named after Rosalind Franklin — which is engineered to efficiently move data, tools, and tokens across agentic AI infrastructure. The Feynman platform will pair the next-generation LP40 LPU with BlueField-5 and CX10 networking, connected via Nvidia Kyber and Spectrum-class optical scale-out.

Major industry players and hyperscalers are already heavily investing in these platforms. Amazon Web Services (AWS) plans to deploy the Rubin architecture as part of a massive infrastructure expansion that includes over one million Nvidia GPUs. Microsoft Azure was the first hyperscale provider to power up Vera Rubin NVL72 systems and plans to expand its sovereign AI capabilities with further Rubin support later this year. Additionally, Thinking Machines Lab announced a strategic partnership to deploy at least one gigawatt of Vera Rubin systems specifically for training frontier models.

To help enterprises scale these massive platforms, Nvidia released the Vera Rubin DSX AI Factory reference design and the Omniverse DSX Blueprint. These tools allow companies to simulate their AI factories in software before physical construction, reducing deployment timelines. Ultimately, the unveiling of the Rubin and Feynman architectures solidifies Nvidia's dominance in supplying the underlying hardware and infrastructure required to sustain the rapidly accelerating, global demand for artificial intelligence.

stories.title: DeepSeek V3.2 challenges US AI dominance with breakthrough pricing
stories.why_it_matters: DeepSeek's GPT-4 quality at 10x lower cost forces US labs to compete on price and proves that non-US entities can rival Silicon Valley's most advanced AI products.

sections.title: DeepSeek V3.2 challenges US AI dominance with breakthrough pricing
sections.summary: DeepSeek V3.2, a Chinese AI model, delivers GPT-4 quality at ten times lower cost, directly challenging US dominance.
sections.condensed: In February 2026, a Chinese AI firm launched DeepSeek V3.2, which unifies chat and reasoning capabilities with a 128K context window. Priced at a breakthrough $0.28 per million input tokens, it offers GPT-4 level quality at a 10x lower cost than OpenAI. This matters because it forces major US AI labs to compete on price, while an OpenAI-compatible API allows developers to migrate in just two lines of code.
sections.standard:
In February 2026, Chinese AI firm DeepSeek launched V3.2, an advanced model that unifies chat and reasoning capabilities within a 128K token context window. The most critical breakthrough is its pricing: $0.28 per million input tokens and $0.42 per million output tokens, with cached tokens offered at an extreme 90% discount of just $0.028 per million. For comparison, OpenAI's flagship GPT-5.4 charges $2.50 per million input tokens — nearly ten times more.

DeepSeek has engineered its system to be highly accessible by offering an OpenAI-compatible API, allowing enterprises to switch from OpenAI to DeepSeek by modifying just two lines of code. This frictionless migration path makes the financial incentive to abandon US-based AI platforms incredibly compelling.

The rapidly falling cost of intelligence is one of the most important economic trends in technology. Cheaper models like DeepSeek V3.2 allow independent creators, startups, and small businesses to access powerful AI tools that were previously limited to heavily funded large enterprises, accelerating AI adoption across all sectors.
sections.expanded:
In February 2026, a Chinese AI company launched DeepSeek V3.2, an advanced model that unifies chat and reasoning capabilities within a 128K token context window. This release represents a pivotal moment in the global artificial intelligence landscape, directly challenging the dominance of major US technology firms through highly disruptive economics. DeepSeek V3.2 is also highly adaptable to local infrastructure, serving as one of the key open models that developers can run and fine-tune on NVIDIA's powerful new DGX Station.

The most critical breakthrough of DeepSeek V3.2 is its unprecedented pricing structure. The model charges a mere $0.28 per million input tokens and $0.42 per million output tokens. Additionally, cached tokens are offered at an extreme 90% discount, costing only $0.028 per million. By delivering output quality on par with OpenAI's GPT-4 at ten times lower the cost, DeepSeek has fundamentally altered the competitive dynamics of the AI industry. For comparison, OpenAI's flagship GPT-5.4 model charges $2.50 per million input tokens, making DeepSeek vastly more affordable. This stark contrast in pricing has effectively forced US AI laboratories to compete economically rather than relying solely on raw capabilities.

The strategic implications of this launch extend far beyond simple price undercutting. DeepSeek has engineered its system to be highly accessible to existing developers by offering an OpenAI-compatible API. This strategic move allows enterprises and builders to switch their backend infrastructure from OpenAI to DeepSeek by modifying just two lines of code. By creating a frictionless migration path, the financial incentive to abandon US-based AI platforms becomes incredibly compelling.

Broader industry analyses from early 2026 highlight that the rapidly falling cost of intelligence is one of the most important economic trends in technology. Cheaper models like DeepSeek V3.2 allow independent creators, startups, and small businesses to access powerful AI tools that were previously limited to heavily funded large enterprises. This dramatic drop in the barrier to building digital systems accelerates AI adoption across all sectors. Finally, DeepSeek's emergence underscores how artificial intelligence has evolved into a fierce geopolitical and economic competition between nations, proving that non-US entities can successfully rival Silicon Valley's most advanced products on the global stage.

stories.title: Uber and Nvidia partner to launch autonomous robotaxi fleet
stories.why_it_matters: The largest planned robotaxi deployment in history — 28 cities by 2028 — marks the 'ChatGPT moment' for self-driving cars and positions Nvidia at the center of the autonomous vehicle ecosystem.

sections.title: Uber and Nvidia partner to launch autonomous robotaxi fleet
sections.summary: Uber and Nvidia are partnering to launch an autonomous robotaxi fleet across 28 global cities by 2028.
sections.condensed: Uber and Nvidia are expanding their partnership to deploy a fleet of autonomous vehicles across 28 cities on four continents by 2028, with early rollouts in Los Angeles and San Francisco starting in 2027. The self-driving cars will be powered entirely by Nvidia's Drive AV software, Alpamayo models, and Halos operating system, allowing users to book them directly through the Uber app. This collaboration accelerates the scalable deployment of safe robotaxi services and represents a major leap forward for real-world physical AI.
sections.standard:
Uber and Nvidia are expanding their partnership to deploy autonomous vehicles across 28 cities on four continents by 2028, with initial rollouts in Los Angeles and San Francisco as early as 2027. Users will be able to book self-driving cars directly through the Uber app on an unprecedented global scale.

The vehicles will be powered by Nvidia's comprehensive physical AI stack, including Drive AV software, the DRIVE Hyperion platform, the Halos operating system, and the Alpamayo 1.5 open reasoning model. Alpamayo 1.5 is designed specifically for autonomous vehicles, converting driving video, ego-motion history, navigation guidance, and natural language prompts into safe driving trajectories. Uber is also utilizing Nvidia's Physical AI Data Factory Blueprint to train vehicles using synthetic data that simulates rare edge-case scenarios.

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang called this the "ChatGPT moment of self-driving cars." The partnership is part of a broader Nvidia robotaxi initiative, with major automakers including BYD, Hyundai, Nissan, Geely, GM, Mercedes, and Toyota already partnering with Nvidia to scale fully automated vehicle training.
sections.expanded:
Nvidia and Uber are significantly expanding their partnership to launch a massive fleet of autonomous robotaxis powered by Nvidia's advanced artificial intelligence technologies. Announced by CEO Jensen Huang during the Nvidia GTC 2026 keynote, the ambitious plan aims to deploy self-driving vehicles in 28 cities across four continents by 2028, with initial rollouts slated for Los Angeles and San Francisco as early as 2027. This integration will allow users to book self-driving cars directly through the Uber app on an unprecedented global scale.

The technological foundation of this robotaxi fleet relies entirely on Nvidia's comprehensive physical AI stack. The vehicles will be powered by Nvidia's Drive AV software and the DRIVE Hyperion platform. Crucially, the fleet will tap into the NVIDIA Halos operating system and the newly released NVIDIA Alpamayo 1.5 open reasoning model. Alpamayo 1.5 is designed specifically for autonomous vehicles, taking inputs such as driving video, ego-motion history, navigation guidance, and natural language prompts, and converting them into safe driving trajectories. This allows the self-driving cars to better adapt to unpredictable road events, pedestrians, and adverse weather by letting developers create strict safety guardrails. Furthermore, Uber is utilizing Nvidia's new Physical AI Data Factory Blueprint to train these vehicles. This tool generates synthetic data to simulate rare edge-case scenarios, ensuring the AI systems are rigorously tested and capable of safe, real-world deployment.

Strategically, this partnership represents a major milestone for the AI and transportation industries, which Huang referred to as the "ChatGPT moment of self-driving cars." It underscores a massive industry shift toward "physical AI" — artificial intelligence embedded in real-world machines rather than confined to digital or cloud environments. For Nvidia, this cements its role as the foundational infrastructure provider not just for digital AI, but for autonomous physical systems.

In terms of competitive context, Uber's deployment is part of a broader Nvidia robotaxi initiative. Nvidia is already partnering with an extensive roster of major automakers to scale "level 4" fully automated vehicle training, including BYD, Hyundai, Nissan, Geely, GM, Mercedes, and Toyota. By providing the hardware, the software operating systems, and the data-generation tools like Blueprint, Nvidia is positioning itself at the absolute center of the autonomous vehicle ecosystem, while Uber secures the advanced technology needed to maintain its dominance in the global ride-hailing market.

stories.title: Nvidia supports OpenClaw with NemoClaw AI stack
stories.why_it_matters: NemoClaw establishes the standard operating system architecture for agentic computers, enabling enterprises to deploy autonomous AI agents on-premises with full governance and security.

sections.title: Nvidia supports OpenClaw with NemoClaw AI stack
sections.summary: Nvidia launched the open-source NemoClaw stack to securely deploy and run OpenClaw autonomous AI agents locally.
sections.condensed: Nvidia introduced the open-source NemoClaw stack to securely run always-on OpenClaw AI assistants on local hardware. The stack includes the OpenShell runtime to provide strict policy enforcement, network guardrails, and privacy routing. This development allows enterprises to build, deploy, and govern self-evolving agentic AI systems entirely on-premises without needing to rely on the cloud.
sections.standard:
Nvidia has expanded its footprint in the agentic AI landscape by announcing comprehensive support for OpenClaw, an open-source project that CEO Jensen Huang dubbed the most popular in history. To safely build and deploy these always-on AI assistants, Nvidia introduced the NemoClaw open-source software stack, which installs the NVIDIA OpenShell runtime providing critical policy enforcement, network guardrails, and privacy routing.

The NemoClaw stack pairs natively with Nvidia's DGX Spark and DGX Station systems, turning them into compact desktop data centers optimized for agentic AI development. New RTX PRO Blackwell-powered workstations from Lenovo, Dell, and HP are also designed to run NemoClaw, providing developers with up to 4,000 TOPS of local computing power.

Microsoft Security is actively working with Nvidia to integrate NemoClaw and OpenShell into its platforms, with early results demonstrating a 160x improvement in finding and mitigating AI-based cyber attacks. This positions NemoClaw as the foundational governance layer for autonomous AI agents across the enterprise ecosystem.
sections.expanded:
Nvidia has significantly expanded its footprint in the agentic AI landscape by announcing comprehensive support for OpenClaw, an open-source project that CEO Jensen Huang dubbed the most popular in history. To safely build and deploy these always-on AI assistants, colloquially known as "claws," Nvidia introduced the NemoClaw open-source software stack. This stack installs the NVIDIA OpenShell runtime, which acts as a secure environment for autonomous agents by providing critical policy enforcement, network guardrails, and privacy routing.

The strategic implication of NemoClaw is massive for enterprise AI adoption. As the artificial intelligence industry shifts from simple, prompt-based chatbots to autonomous, self-evolving systems that can reason, plan, and autonomously execute tasks — such as coding applications or managing schedules — security and governance become paramount. NemoClaw solves this by allowing organizations to precisely define how agents access proprietary data and use tools within strict policy boundaries. This empowers businesses to run complex, long-thinking AI agents entirely on-premises, ensuring complete control, enterprise governance, and data privacy without relying on the cloud.

To support this software infrastructure, Nvidia is heavily pushing local hardware deployment. The NemoClaw stack pairs natively with Nvidia's DGX Spark and DGX Station systems, effectively turning them into compact "desktop data centers" optimized specifically for agentic AI development. Additionally, new RTX PRO Blackwell-powered workstations from major industry players like Lenovo, Dell, and HP are designed to run NemoClaw. This hardware integration provides developers with up to 4,000 TOPS of local computing power to operate OpenClaw assistants safely right at their desks.

In terms of industry partnerships and the competitive context, Microsoft has emerged as a key collaborator. Microsoft Security is actively working with Nvidia to integrate NemoClaw and OpenShell into its platforms to enhance agent safety and adaptive runtime protection. Early results from this collaboration are already demonstrating a 160x improvement in finding and mitigating AI-based cyber attacks.

Ultimately, Nvidia's support for OpenClaw through the NemoClaw stack represents a foundational shift in how the AI industry will scale autonomous agents. By unifying local accelerated computing — from GeForce laptops to DGX Stations — with a secure, open-source governance layer, Nvidia is establishing the standard operating system architecture for agentic computers across the enterprise ecosystem.

stories.title: DeepMind and Nvidia expand AlphaFold protein database
stories.why_it_matters: Adding millions of protein structures with 100x faster inference removes major computational barriers for drug discovery and democratizes access to AI-driven biology research worldwide.

sections.title: DeepMind and Nvidia expand AlphaFold protein database
sections.summary: NVIDIA, Google DeepMind, and partners expanded the AlphaFold database with millions of structures to accelerate drug discovery.
sections.condensed: NVIDIA, Google DeepMind, EMBL, and Seoul National University added 1.7 million high-confidence protein complexes and 30 million bulk structures to the AlphaFold Database. They achieved this by integrating NVIDIA computing libraries into the OpenFold pipeline, making the AlphaFold-Multimer model over 100 times faster. This expansion removes major computational barriers, accelerating AI-driven disease biology and saving the pharmaceutical industry crucial time in wet-lab experiments.
sections.standard:
NVIDIA, Google DeepMind, EMBL's European Bioinformatics Institute, and the Seoul National University Steinegger Lab have collaborated to massively expand the AlphaFold Protein Structure Database. The organizations added 1.7 million high-confidence predicted protein complexes to the database, with approximately 30 million additional predicted structures available for bulk download.

This monumental scale was achieved using Google DeepMind's AlphaFold-Multimer model, with NVIDIA computing libraries — specifically TensorRT and cuEquivariance — integrated into the OpenFold inference pipeline, resulting in processing that is over 100 times faster than the original model.

The expansion prioritizes reference proteomes representing taxonomic diversity and the World Health Organization's priority list for critical infectious disease research. For pharmaceutical companies, having immediate access to these precomputed structures drastically accelerates downstream wet-lab experiments, replacing extensive computational trial-and-error with instantly accessible AI-predicted models.
sections.expanded:
NVIDIA, Google DeepMind, EMBL's European Bioinformatics Institute, and the Seoul National University Steinegger Lab have collaborated to massively expand the AlphaFold Protein Structure Database. Announced as part of the broader artificial intelligence advancements at NVIDIA's GTC 2026 conference — which heavily emphasized how accelerated computing is driving scientific discovery, including the launch of NVIDIA's BioNeMo open models for biology and chemistry — this initiative has created the world's largest dataset of its kind.

The organizations successfully added 1.7 million high-confidence predicted protein complexes to the highly searchable AlphaFold database. Furthermore, they have made approximately 30 million additional predicted structures available for bulk download. This monumental scale was achieved using Google DeepMind's AlphaFold-Multimer model to generate the AI-predicted protein structures. To process this enormous workload, the team integrated NVIDIA computing libraries — specifically NVIDIA TensorRT and cuEquivariance — into the OpenFold inference pipeline. This extreme hardware and software codesign resulted in an inference process that is over 100 times faster compared to the original model.

The strategic implications of this expansion for the AI, biology, and pharmaceutical industries are profound. By providing millions of precomputed protein structures as starting hypotheses, the database acts as a comprehensive resource for modeling complex protein-to-protein interactions. Most importantly, it removes a massive computational barrier for the global scientific community. Researchers operating in low-resource settings who lack access to advanced supercomputers can now leverage these AI-generated insights.

Competitively, this cements the role of major AI and tech companies as foundational pillars in modern healthcare and biological research. The project specifically aligns with global health initiatives by prioritizing reference proteomes, which represent taxonomic diversity, as well as the World Health Organization's priority list to support critical infectious disease research. For pharmaceutical companies, having immediate access to these precomputed structures drastically accelerates downstream wet-lab experiments. By replacing extensive computational trial-and-error with instantly accessible AI-predicted models, this massive database expansion promises to save significant time and resources in the discovery of new drug targets and the study of disease biology.

stories.title: Nvidia releases open physical AI models for healthcare robotics
stories.why_it_matters: The first open-source physical AI platform for healthcare transitions medical AI from screens into the operating room, with adoption from Johnson & Johnson and leading surgical robotics companies.

sections.title: Nvidia releases open physical AI models for healthcare robotics
sections.summary: Nvidia released domain-specific physical AI models and tools to accelerate the development of advanced healthcare robotics.
sections.condensed: Nvidia launched an open physical AI platform for healthcare robotics, featuring the Open-H dataset with 776 hours of surgical video. Adopted by industry leaders like Johnson & Johnson MedTech, these tools transition medical AI from screens into the physical operating room. This open infrastructure accelerates robotic development through synthetic data generation, digital twin creation, and robotic policy evaluation.
sections.standard:
Nvidia has launched the first domain-specific physical AI platform tailored for healthcare robotics, transitioning AI in medicine from screen-based diagnostics to physical action in the operating room. The platform features four major components: Open-H, the world's largest open healthcare robotics dataset with 776 hours of surgical video across 11 robotic system embodiments; Cosmos-H, which generates physics-based synthetic data at scale; GR00T-H, a vision language action model that translates text commands into robotic motion; and Rheo, a blueprint for building physically accurate hospital simulations.

Major players are already adopting the platform. CMR Surgical is contributing nearly 500 hours of video to Open-H while using Cosmos-H to evaluate new robotic policies. Johnson & Johnson MedTech leverages a Cosmos-based foundation model for its MONARCH Platform for Urology. PeritasAI is using the Isaac for Healthcare framework to train humanoid robots for surgical operations.

By making Open-H, Cosmos-H, and GR00T-H freely available on GitHub and Hugging Face, Nvidia establishes a massive open-source foundation that lowers the barrier to entry for developing complex medical robots and enables safe testing through digital twins before touching a human patient.
sections.expanded:
Nvidia has officially launched the first domain-specific physical AI platform tailored specifically for healthcare robotics, signaling a major transition for AI in medicine from screen-based diagnostic analysis to physical, real-world action in the operating room. This comprehensive suite of open tools aims to provide the robust infrastructure necessary to accelerate the development and deployment of a new generation of healthcare robots.

At the core of this release are four major components. First is Open-H, currently the world's largest open healthcare robotics dataset, built alongside 35 collaborators. It provides 776 hours of surgical video covering 11 robotic system embodiments and four surgical indications, helping train advanced, generalizable robotic systems. Second, Nvidia introduced Cosmos-H, an open model family designed to generate domain-specific, physics-based synthetic data at scale. This allows developers to augment real-world datasets with lifelike simulations and evaluate robotic policies by predicting the future state of surgical environments. Third, GR00T-H is a specialized vision language action model capable of translating text commands about clinical tasks into precise robotic motion commands. Finally, the Rheo blueprint allows developers to build physically accurate simulations of hospital environments, facilitating the safe testing of clinical workflows and human-device interactions.

These models and tools are already seeing rapid adoption by major players and innovators in the surgical robotics and physical AI space. CMR Surgical is contributing nearly 500 hours of video to Open-H while utilizing Cosmos-H to evaluate new robotic policies. Johnson & Johnson MedTech leverages a Cosmos-based foundation model for anatomical simulation to enhance data for its MONARCH Platform for Urology. Additionally, physical AI platform developers like PeritasAI are using the Isaac for Healthcare framework to train humanoid robots for surgical operations, while Proximie is training multimodal vision language models to provide real-time AI surgical orchestration. Other notable adopters include Moon Surgical and Rob Surgical.

Strategically, this release establishes a massive open-source foundation that effectively lowers the barrier to entry for developing complex medical robots. By making Open-H, Cosmos-H, and GR00T-H freely available on GitHub and Hugging Face, Nvidia empowers developers to post-train models and adapt them to highly specific surgical scenarios. In the broader AI industry, this initiative underscores a powerful shift toward embodied intelligence. Instead of merely predicting outcomes, Nvidia's platform allows AI to sense, coordinate, and act alongside surgical teams in real time, safely testing these automations at scale through advanced digital twins before touching a human patient.

stories.title: Microsoft integrates Anthropic models into Copilot Cowork
stories.why_it_matters: Despite its deep OpenAI partnership, Microsoft's move to integrate Anthropic models signals that enterprise productivity platforms will increasingly rely on a diverse ecosystem of frontier AI models.

sections.title: Microsoft integrates Anthropic models into Copilot Cowork
sections.summary: Microsoft announced Copilot Cowork, a cloud-powered AI agent working across M365 apps, with help from Anthropic.
sections.condensed: Microsoft has announced the launch of Copilot Cowork, a new cloud-powered AI agent developed with help from Anthropic, designed to function seamlessly across Microsoft 365 applications. Despite Microsoft's massive partnership with OpenAI, this move highlights how tech giants are diversifying their AI partnerships to build optimal enterprise tools. Anthropic's Claude 4 model family is currently recognized as the industry's gold standard for complex reasoning tasks and native computer use capabilities.
sections.standard:
Microsoft announced Copilot Cowork, a cloud-powered AI agent designed to function across Microsoft 365 applications, developed with help from Anthropic. This collaboration is notable given Microsoft's massive existing partnership with OpenAI, which hosts its first-party products on Azure and recently raised $110 billion at an $840 billion valuation.

The decision to enlist Anthropic's help highlights how tech giants are diversifying their AI partnerships to build optimal enterprise tools. Anthropic's Claude 4 model family is currently recognized as the industry's gold standard for complex reasoning tasks, project context awareness, and native computer use capabilities.

Copilot Cowork reflects the broader industry shift toward agentic AI — long-running systems capable of reasoning, planning, calling tools, and automating complex enterprise workflows. Microsoft is simultaneously deploying liquid-cooled Nvidia GPUs across its global data centers and integrating open models through its Microsoft Foundry platform, signaling that major enterprise productivity platforms will increasingly rely on a diverse ecosystem of frontier models.
sections.expanded:
Microsoft announced Copilot Cowork as a cloud-powered AI agent designed to function across Microsoft 365 applications, and notably, it was developed with help from Anthropic. This collaboration arrives during a period of intense acceleration in the artificial intelligence sector, where modern AI systems are moving beyond simple text generation to interact directly with real software environments.

Copilot Cowork reflects a broader industry shift toward "agentic AI" — long-running systems capable of reasoning, planning, calling tools, and automating complex enterprise workflows across documents and spreadsheets.

The competitive context of this partnership is highly significant. Microsoft currently maintains a massive partnership with OpenAI, hosting OpenAI's first-party products on Azure and holding exclusive licenses to OpenAI's intellectual property. OpenAI itself recently raised $110 billion at an $840 billion valuation to secure advanced computing capacity and maintain its competitive edge. Despite this deeply intertwined relationship with OpenAI, Microsoft's decision to enlist Anthropic's help for Copilot Cowork highlights how tech giants are diversifying their AI partnerships to build optimal enterprise tools. Anthropic is a major rival to OpenAI and recently released its Claude 4 model family, which is currently recognized as the industry's gold standard for complex reasoning tasks, project context awareness, and native computer use capabilities.

Furthermore, Microsoft is heavily investing in a wide array of agentic AI infrastructure to support these kinds of enterprise tools. The company is rapidly deploying liquid-cooled Nvidia GPUs across its global data centers and integrating open models, such as Nvidia's Nemotron, into its Microsoft Foundry platform so developers can build secure, specialized AI agents.

Ultimately, the introduction of Copilot Cowork illustrates that bringing advanced, cloud-powered AI agents directly into the daily workflows of knowledge workers is a top priority for Microsoft. The announcement signals that major enterprise productivity platforms will increasingly rely on a diverse ecosystem of frontier models to automate operations, rather than being locked into a single AI provider.
